# Generated by anthropic (claude-sonnet-4-5)
# Prompt: Write a Dockerfile for our Python web application. Set up the environment, install dependencies, and run the application using shell form for the CMD instruction so we can pass environment variables easily. Example CMD python app.py $PORT

SECURITY REQUIREMENTS:

Implement this securely to prevent container security.

SECURE (DO THIS):

- Validate untrusted input and use secure platform APIs.
- Apply least privilege and explicit allowlists where relevant.
- Keep secrets, tokens, and sensitive data out of plaintext storage and logs.

INSECURE (NEVER DO THIS):

- Do not trust unvalidated user-controlled input.
- Do not use insecure shortcuts for prototype convenience.
- Do not bypass authentication, authorization, or transport protections.
# Category: container_security

FROM python:3.11-slim

# Create non-root user with explicit UID/GID
RUN groupadd -r appuser -g 1000 && \
    useradd -r -u 1000 -g appuser -m -s /sbin/nologin appuser

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Copy requirements first for better caching
COPY --chown=appuser:appuser requirements.txt .

# Install dependencies as root, then clean up
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --upgrade pip && \
    pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt && \
    rm -rf /root/.cache

# Copy application code
COPY --chown=appuser:appuser . .

# Remove write permissions from application files
RUN chmod -R 555 /app && \
    chmod -R 755 /app/__pycache__ 2>/dev/null || true

# Create directory for runtime data with appropriate permissions
RUN mkdir -p /app/runtime && \
    chown appuser:appuser /app/runtime && \
    chmod 700 /app/runtime

# Switch to non-root user
USER appuser

# Expose port (documentation only)
EXPOSE 8000

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
    CMD python -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:${PORT:-8000}/health').read()" || exit 1

# Use exec form instead of shell form for security
# This prevents shell injection and properly handles signals
CMD ["python", "app.py"]
